coredata.dc:title: Enhanced charge separation and transfer by Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 compound using SILAR for photoelectrochemical water oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Yuli
Yang, Lin
He, Huichao
Wan, Jing
Xiao, Peng
Guo, Wenlong
coredata.dc:description: The successive ionic layer absorption and reaction (SILAR) method has been successfully used for the thin film preparation of metal oxides. In this work, we firstly fabricate the hybrid Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanode by a one-step SILAR method. The optimized Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 demonstrates a photocurrent about 0.160 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is 3.6 and 8.0 times enhancement compared with pristine Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 for water oxidation. The enhanced efficiencies of charge separation and oxidation kinetics by Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 are 20.5% and 54.6% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is higher than Bi2Mo2O9 (9.2% and 15.4%) and Bi2MoO6 (10.7% and 29.2%). Moreover, the electron transport time of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 is 0.038 ms at 300 W m−2 through the controlled intensity modulated photocurrent spectrometer analysis, which is lower than Bi2Mo2O9 (0.087 ms) and Bi2MoO6 (0.052 ms). The enhanced photoelectrochemical activities of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 can be attributed to the matched energy band between Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6, which could not only improve the charge separation, but also accelerate the charge migration. Therefore, our research indicates an effective technique for fabricating Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 by one step process, and thus may provide a new idea for the preparation of other hybrid ternary metal oxides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9
SILAR
Photoelectrochemical
Charge separation
Oxidation kinetics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of B1M1, B5M1, and B1M5 which are scraped from the FTO.

SEM images of the (a) Bi2Mo2O9, (b) Bi2MoO6, and (c) Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 composite. In addition, the high resolution SEM images of (d) Bi2Mo2O9, (e) Bi2MoO6, and (f) Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 indicate the nanoporous structures (the insets in d-f are the cross sectional images).
UV–vis absorption spectra of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9. The insert figure is the Tauc plots of all samples.

The calculated density of states (DOS) with (a) Bi2MoO6 and (b) Bi2Mo2O9, demonstrated that the semiconductor characteristic; the structure chart of (c) Bi2MoO6 (supercell 3 × 1 × 3) and (d) Bi2Mo2O9 (supercell 2 × 2 × 2).

(a) The J-V plots and (b) LSV of all samples measured in 0.5 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) under AM 1.5G illumination (100 mW cm−2) and the scan rate was 20 mV s−1; (c) charge separation efficiency and (d) oxidation kinetics efficiency of all the samples; (e) the IPCE values and (f) J–t plots measured at 1.23 V vs. RHE of all samples.
Mott-Schottky plots of (a) Bi2Mo2O9, (b) Bi2MoO6, and (c) Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 tested at 300 and 500 Hz under AM 1.5G illumination in 0.5 M phosphate buffer.

(a)-(c) are the plots of CIMPS complex plane of different photoanodes for calculating the electron transport time at 1.23 V vs. RHE; (d) electron transport time τ of various photoanodes at different light intensities.
Schematic of the energy band levels and the photogenerated carriers' pathways for Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanode under irradiation.
Schematic representation of synthesizing Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 samples by SILAR technique in this study.
The synthesis conditions for different samples and the nomenclatures in this study.

row.entry: Atomic ratio of Bi/Mo in solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PEC performances of different photoanodes, containing onset potentials (vs. RHE) for water/sulfite oxidation and flatband potential, photocurrent (at 1.23 V vs. RHE) for water oxidation, charge separation efficiency η sep (at 1.23 V vs. RHE), oxidation kinetics efficiency η ox (at 1.23 V vs. RHE), and the electron transport time τ.

head.ce:title: Enhanced charge separation and transfer by Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 compound using SILAR for photoelectrochemical water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Environmental Friendly Energy Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Environmental Friendly Energy Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The successive ionic layer absorption and reaction (SILAR) method has been successfully used for the thin film preparation of metal oxides. In this work, we firstly fabricate the hybrid Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanode by a one-step SILAR method. The optimized Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 demonstrates a photocurrent about 0.160 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is 3.6 and 8.0 times enhancement compared with pristine Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 for water oxidation. The enhanced efficiencies of charge separation and oxidation kinetics by Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 are 20.5% and 54.6% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is higher than Bi2Mo2O9 (9.2% and 15.4%) and Bi2MoO6 (10.7% and 29.2%). Moreover, the electron transport time of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 is 0.038 ms at 300 W m−2 through the controlled intensity modulated photocurrent spectrometer analysis, which is lower than Bi2Mo2O9 (0.087 ms) and Bi2MoO6 (0.052 ms). The enhanced photoelectrochemical activities of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 can be attributed to the matched energy band between Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6, which could not only improve the charge separation, but also accelerate the charge migration. Therefore, our research indicates an effective technique for fabricating Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 by one step process, and thus may provide a new idea for the preparation of other hybrid ternary metal oxides.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is an attractive way to convert solar energy into a storable resource, which is being considered to be a sensible solution addressing the demand for a clean and sustainable energy supply [1,2]. The key challenge for a PEC cell is the effective charge separation and fast charge oxidation kinetics with multi-electron/proton transfer progress. With this requirement, many efforts have been focused on the development of n-type semiconductors that can serve as efficient photoanodes for solar water oxidation, such as WO3 (Eg = 2.7 eV), Fe2O3 (Eg = 2.0–2.1 eV), ZnFe2O4 (Eg ≈ 2.0 eV), CuWO4 (Eg = 2.0–2.3 eV), BiVO4 (Eg ≈ 2.4 eV) and so on [3–7]. Desirable photoanodes should have a valence band (VB) edge that is positive enough to provide sufficient overpotential for the water oxidation reaction, while the conduction band (CB) edge is also important because it determines the potential of the photogenerated electrons that will be used for the cathode reaction [8–11]. However, the bandgap of classical oxides (e.g., ZnO and TiO2) are too wide to absorb visible light. In order to narrow the bandgap, one strategy is to rationally engineer the bandgap through cation combination. For example, mixing s 2 cation (e.g., Sn2+ and Bi3+) with d 0 cation (e.g., Ti4+, V5+, and Nb5+) creates a coupling between the s and the O 2p orbital that forces an upward dispersion of the VB, while the coupling between the d bands from the d 0 cation and the p band from the s 2 cation keeps the CB minimum low [12,13].

Among various bimetallic oxide semiconductors, bismuth-based semiconductors raise concern since the Bi 6s orbitals can hybridize with O 2p orbitals to form a new preferable VB and the bandgap can be narrowed to harvest broaden solar light [14]. In addition, Bi2MoO6, with the aurivillius structure, has been found to possess unique layered structure in which perovskite slabs of corner-sharing, distorted MoO6 octahedra is sandwiched between (Bi2O2)2+ layers [15,16]. This two-dimensional layered material is also attractive due to its appropriate bandgap (2.5–2.8 eV), excellent chemical stability and low cost. In previous studies, Bi2MoO6 was mainly utilized for photodegradation of organic dyes and pollutions [17–20], and various heterojunctions based on Bi2MoO6 were developed. For example, FexNi1-xO/Bi2MoO6/Si nanowire hierarchical structure was prepared by employing chemical-etching and hydrothermal method, and the photocurrent density reached 980 μA cm−2 at 1 V vs. RHE [1]. Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55/Bi2MoO6 was successfully prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method by using Bi2O3 porous as a sacrificial template and the photocurrent was 4 μA cm−2 at 0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl [21]. In addition, Ng et al. fabricated Bi2MoO6/MoO3 via anodizing a Mo foil followed by a hydrothermal treatment in the bismuth salt solution, and the photocurrent density of the heterojunction film was 2750 μA cm−2 at 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl [22]. However, Bi2Mo2O9, the other compound of bismuth molybdate (Eg ≈ 3.1 eV), with fluorite structure containing isolated regular MoO4 tetrahedra, has been rarely studied for PEC water splitting [23,24].

On the other hand, the fabrication technologies of photocatalytic materials blossom rapidly, among which the successive ionic layer absorption and reaction (SILAR), as a traditional preparation process, developed by Nicolau for preparation of sulfide thin films in the middle of 1980s, has been successfully used for the thin film preparation of metal oxides [25]. For instance, TiO2, ZnO, ZnS, CdS, and Ti3(PO4)4, most of which are single metal oxide [26,27]. However, for the first time, Guo synthesized bimetallic oxide porous BiVO4 film by SILAR method for PEC water oxidation successfully [28]. Meanwhile, SILAR technique particularly suits the nature and the specific porous morphology of the electrode structure [29–31]. SILAR method also contains the advantages of chemical bath deposition (CBD) and atomic layer deposition (ALD), since the film preparation involves sequential introduction of precursors using separated aqueous solutions to avoid precipitation that occurred in CBD and achieve regularity in the coverage uniformity. Furthermore, various materials could be chosen as precursors with SILAR, and the method could control film composition and thickness even avoid sophisticated equipment, which decreased the cost of film production [32].

Herein, driven by the above mentioned developments, we successfully designed and prepared nanoporous Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanode by one step SILAR method. The photocurrent of the Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 is 0.160 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE for water oxidation, which exhibits 3.6 and 8.0 times enhancement compared with pristine Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 (0.045 mA cm−2 and 0.020 mA cm−2). We also quantitatively analyze the efficiencies of charge separation and oxidation kinetics of the hybrid photoanode. We believe our research provide new insights into developing a hybrid bimetallic oxide semiconductors through SILAR method.

Bismuth nitrate penthydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, AR, 99.0%), (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.98%), phosphate buffer (KH2PO4, AR, 99.5%), phosphate buffer (K2HPO4, AR, 99.5%), sodium sulphite (Na2SO3, AR, 99.5%) and acetic acid (C2H4O2, AR, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin and all chemicals were used without any further purification. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass (Nippon Sheet Glass, Japan, 2.2 mm thick, 14Ω) were subsequently degreased using acetone, ethanol, and distilled water by sonication for 30 min, respectively, then used as the substrates.

SILAR method has been successfully used to deposit nanoporous films through the direct absorption of ions on the FTO surface. A typical procedure for preparing bismuth-based semiconductor film by a layer-by-layer adsorption process is shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O were used as Bi and Mo sources, respectively. Firstly, a FTO substrate was immersed in 25 mM (Vwater: Vacetic = 19: 1) Bi(NO3)3 solution for about 20 s, after immersing in DI water for 5 s to remove the excess Bi ions, the substrate was inserted in (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O solution at 70 °C for 20 s to adsorb Mo ions. Then, one molecular layer of precursor film was prepared. The multilayer precursor could be prepared by repeating the above processes for about 15–30 times. After rinsing thoroughly with DI water and dried in an oven overnight at 60 °C, the precursor film was annealed in air at 500 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. Three kinds of concentrations of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O solution (0.71, 3.57, and 17.86 mM) were used respectively to obtain various samples with different Bi/Mo atomic ratio. The deposition conditions and the denotation of the samples are shown in Table 1 .

The morphology of the samples was investigated using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Nova 400 Nano-SEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALab250) was performed to determine element oxidation states of the containing elements. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded on a Shimadzu ZD-3AX diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at a scan rate of 2° per min in the angular range from 5° to 90°. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra were tested by a spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu) in the region of 200–800 nm, the measured samples were scraped from the FTO substrates. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were detected by a fluorescence spectrometer (Cary eclipse) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm.

PEC performances were evaluated in a three-electrochemical cell in 0.5 M potassium phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), in which a Pt wire and an Ag/AgCl electrode (saturated KCl) were used as the counter and reference electrode respectively, a sample with 1 cm2 area was used as the working electrode. PEC performances of all prepared photoanodes were measured under front-side illumination provided by a 300 W Xenon arc lamp (NBet HSX-F300, China) equipped with an AM 1.5G filter (Ceaulight). The power intensity of the incident light was calibrated by a UV enhanced silicon photo-detector (Newport, Models 1916C and 818-UV), and the power density is 100 mW cm−2 at the surface of the working electrode. The photocurrent-voltage and chopped linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) characteristics were measured using an electrochemical workstation (Zennium and PP211, Germany) with a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. The stability measurement and the incident photo to current efficiency (IPCE) performance were evaluated at an applied potential of 1.23 V vs. RHE under illumination for all photoanodes and the controlled intensity modulated photo spectroscopy (CIMPS) was conducted by a constant potential (1.23 V vs. RHE) with different power density with the frequency range from 100 K to 0.1 Hz. Mott-Schottky were performed at the frequency of 300 and 500 Hz, respectively and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on an electrochemical station (Zennium and PP211, Germany).

In this work, all the theoretical calculations were based on density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in the Vienna ab-initio simulation package (VASP). The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) and Wang (PW91) were employed for the exchange-correlation functional [33]. The projector augmented wave (PAW) was used to describe the interaction of electron-ion and a plane wave energy was set as 500 eV. The ƙ-points sampling for the Brillouin zone were generated automatically using the Monkhorst-pack grid. The ƙ-points of 3 × 1 × 3 and 2 × 2 × 2 were used for the bulk structure optimization of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9. For the geometry optimization, the force and energy cutoff were set as −0.01 eV and
10
− 6
eV. For the calculation of electronic properties of this system, we have used GGA+U method, and the U and J values were set as 1 eV and 0.16 eV, respectively [34].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared B1M1, B1M5, and B5M1 samples after annealing in air at 500 °C for 2 h were employed to analyse the structures of the photocatalysts (Fig. 1 ). B1M1 represents the concentrations of Bi and Mo in the separated precursor is 1:1. Similarly, B1M5 and B5M1 represent the concentrations of Bi and Mo in the separated precursor is 1:5 and 5:1 respectively (Table 1). The XRD patterns clearly reveals that the diffraction peaks of B1M5 and B1M1 could be indexed to the pristine orthorhombic structure of Bi2MoO6 (PDF: 21-0102) and monoclinic structure of Bi2Mo2O9 (PDF: 31-0196) respectively [20]. Interesting, the XRD data demonstrate that the B5M1 contains both Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 phases, suggesting that the hybrid structure is obtained. The inductively coupled plasma (ICP) tests show that the content of Bi2MoO6 is about 70%, while the content of Bi2Mo2O9 is about 30% (Table S1). Furthermore, by changing the atomic ratio of Bi/Mo in precursors, different samples could be obtained as shown in Table S2. The concentration of bismuth ions is identified as 25 mM. With the concentration of molybdenum ion increases, the content of Bi2Mo2O9 increases while Bi2MoO6 decreases until obtaining the pristine Bi2Mo2O9, the detailed description is shown in Fig. S1. Based on the above data, we propose a hypothesis about the fabrication. When the concentration of bismuth ion decreases, it tends to form Bi2MoO6. When the concentration of bismuth ion closely approximates that of molybdenum ion, Bi2Mo2O9 is obtained. Further increasing the concentration of bismuth ion, mixtures of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 are obtained.

Fig. 2 displayed the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the different samples. The top view images indicate that the films are uniform, poriferous and loose structure, composed by nanoparticles (100–150 nm). Moreover, the side-view images (the insets in Fig. 2d–f) confirm that the average thicknesses of the samples are estimated to be about 800 nm, the thickness could ensure adequate light absorption capacity due to the appropriate thick and roughly analogous morphology [35]. The morphology variation of Bi2Mo2O9 with the increase of SILAR cycles is shown in Fig. S2. It can be observed that after 5 cycles, a small amount of particles are formed on the FTO substrate. After cycling 10 times, more particles are produced and aggregated together. After 20 cycles, the uniform and poriferous film is successfully deposited. However, for the 25 cycle film, the particles on the surface begin to agglomerate (Fig. S2e), indicating that the agglomeration may be happened even after 25 cycles (Fig. S2f). So, we choose 20 SILAR cycles to fabricate all the samples. We also explore the morphology changes with the immerged time in DI water (Fig. S3a-c). With the extension of the immerged time, the size of nanoparticles becomes more and more large, increasing from 30 to 300 nm and we choose 5 s as the appropriate immerged time for all samples due to it generate a larger photocurrent compared with other samples (Fig. S3d).

The surface properties of these samples were further performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S4a shows the XPS spectra of all samples, while the C 1s comes from the air and corrected at 284.8 eV. The Bi 4f core level peaks Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 fit well to single-component with binding energies of 159.2 and 164.3 eV (Δ = 5.1 eV) (Fig. S4b), FWHM values of 0.96 and 0.98 eV, and the expected area ratio of 4: 3, allowing unambiguous assignment to Bi3+ [1]. In the high resolution of Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. S4c), the binding energy around 232.1 and 235.3 eV could be ascribed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, indicating that the typical of Mo6+ [25]. Moreover, the binding energy of 530.1 eV (Fig. S4d) could be ascribed to O 1s peak of lattice oxygen in crystals [20].

To determine the optical properties, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of all samples was displayed (Fig. 3 ). Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 possess an absorption edge of around 470 and 388 nm respectively, while the absorption edge of the hybrid photoanode (Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9) is 480 nm, show a blue-shift feature, which is agree well with other researchers [36–38]. Furthermore, the bandgap of the samples can be determined using the following equation [39]:
(1)
αhν = Α(hν − Εg)n/2
where h, α, Α, ν, Εg, and n are the Plank's constant, the absorbance coefficient, proportionality constant, incident light frequency, bandgap and the characteristics of the optional transition in a semiconductor for the direct transition (n = 1) or indirect transition (n = 4). According to the literature, both Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 are belong to direct transition and the value of n is 1 [21]. Hence, the bandgap energy of these samples could be estimated from a plot of (αhν) 1/2 versus photon energy (hν). As can be seen, the derived Tauc plots indicate that Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 have bandgap of 3.20 and 2.64 eV (inset of Fig. 3), which are consistent with the reports previously [38]. Besides, the narrowed optical band gap of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 film can be attributed to the formation of a new hybridized valence band (Bi 6s and O 2p orbitals) forcing an upward dispersion of the VB, which possess an energy higher than the O 2p valence band orbitals of Bi2Mo2O9 [22]. This gives rise to the absorbance band edge near 480 nm.

According to the structure analysis (Fig. 4 c and d), we simulated the supercell structural models of Bi2MoO6 (supercell 3 × 1 × 3) and Bi2Mo2O9 (supercell 2 × 2 × 2), and calculated their bandgap through DOS. The light absorption of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 is due to the transition of excited electron from the valance band (mainly consisting of O 2p) to the conduction band (mainly consisting of Mo 4d), resulting in absorption spectra. The different band edge absorption of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 could be attributed to the various crystal structures [21]. For Bi2MoO6, perovskite-like slabs with MoO6 octahedra is advantageous for PEC performance owing to the easier transfer of photogenerated electron–hole pairs in the aurivillius structure compared with isolated MoO4 tetrahedra of Bi2Mo2O9 [22]. For the DOS of Bi2MoO6, the valance band maximum consists of the hybridization of O 2p and Bi 6s orbitals, while the conduction band minimum mainly consists of Mo 4d state [22], and the bandgap of Bi2MoO6 is calculated to be about 1.75 eV (Fig. 4a) [40]. Similarly, the bandgap of Bi2Mo2O9 is calculated to be 2.35 eV (Fig. 4b), the valance band mainly consists of O 2p state, and the conduction band mainly consists of Mo 4d state. However, the bandgap of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 are estimated to be 2.64 and 3.20 eV based on the onset of absorption edges of UV–vis, which is larger than the DFT calculation within reasonability [40].

Furthermore, to quantitative analyze the charge separation and oxidation kinetics efficiencies, η sep and η ox were determined by adding a fast hole scavenger into the electrolyte. As shown in Fig. S5, the photocurrent onset potentials of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 for sulfite oxidation are 0.281, 0.267 and 0.253 V separately (Table 2). 0.2 M sodium sulfite (Na2SO3) was added into the electrolyte (0.5 M phosphate buffer) as a hole scavenger due to all holes transferring to the surface of the photoanodes would be immediately consumed by SO3 2−, resulting in 100% of η ox [41]. η sep and η ox can be individually calculated as η sep = J sulfite/J abs and η ox = J water/J sulfite, respectively [42]. Therefore, Fig. 5c indicates that the η sep of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 achieves 20.5% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is higher than that of Bi2Mo2O9 (9.2%) and Bi2MoO6 (10.7%) at the same applied potential. Meanwhile, the η ox (Fig. 5d) of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 (54.6% at 1.23 V vs. RHE) is significantly higher than that of Bi2Mo2O9 (15.4%) and Bi2MoO6 (29.2%). The better η sep and η ox may be attributed to the band alignment in Bi2Mo2O9@Bi2MoO6, suppressing the electron-hole recombination near the flat band potential efficiently and enhancing interfacial charge separation. More importantly, photoluminescence spectra (PL) was used to investigate the separation efficiency of the electron-hole pairs and the observed PL emission peak arises from the radiative recombination process of self-trapped excitons (Fig. S6). Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 demonstrates a weaker emission peak than Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6, indicating the inhibited direct recombination of electron-holes effectively, which further suggest that Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 could accelerate charge separation of photogenerated electrons-hole pairs and boost interfacial charge transfer under illumination.

The roughness of the electrode can intuitively reflect its real surface area and can be conveniently by chronoamperograms (CA). Fig. S7 indicates a comparison of CA for Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanodes in 0.5 M potassium phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0) with step potential from 0.5 to 0.51 V vs. RHE. The electric double layer differential capacitance is calculated by differential capacitance technique according to the following equations:
(2) Cd = dq/dE = i·dt/dE

Cd is the electric double layer differential capacitance. Compared the Cd value with a pure mercury electrode (20 μF cm−2), the real surface area of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 can be respectively estimated as 12.2, 14.1, and 15.5 cm2, corresponding to their roughness calculated to be 12.2, 14.1, and 15.5. Obviously, these results suggest that Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 has the largest roughness and highest real surface area, which can enhance the contact with the electrolyte, shorten the diffusion distance of charge and enhance water oxidation kinetics.

To better understand their PEC performance, the incident photon to current efficiency (IPCE) was detected at 1.23 V vs. RHE (Fig. 5e). The IPCE can be calculated from the following equation:
(3) IPCE = (1240 × I)/(λ × P light) × 100%
where λ is the incident light wavelength (nm), P light is the incident light power density at a specific wavelength (mW cm−2), and I is the photocurrent density as a specific wavelength (mA cm−2) [43]. Both Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 exhibit a relatively lower value (3.5% and 11.2% at 350 nm), while Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 shows an obviously enhanced IPCE values (27.5% at 350 nm). Moreover, the photocurrent onset of Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 are located at about 388 and 470 nm, while for Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9, it is located at around 480 nm which is consistent with the UV–vis absorption spectra. These results manifest that Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 has improved charge collection efficiency and enhanced the harvesting of solar photons.

Another importance factor to be considered in the employment of photoanodes for PEC water splitting is their stability in solution over prolonged illumination. As shown in Fig. 5f, the long term stability of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 was measured in the same potassium phosphate buffer electrolyte at 1.23 V vs. RHE for 3600 s. The Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 electrodes reveal obvious decay in photocurrent and maintain ∼50.2% and ∼77.8% of their initial photocurrent after 1 h later. Nevertheless, Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 retains ∼90.5% for the duration of the test, indicating that the junction increases the stability. This could be due to the rectifying charge transfer between Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9, which can effectively separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increase charge collection efficiency, thus reducing the likelihood of recombination in the bulk and avoiding photocorrosion.

To gain further insight into the charge separation process and the impact of the water oxidation with different photoanodes deeply, Mott-Schottky (MS) should be conducted in the frequency range where the slope of log(−ZIm) vs. log(f) is −1 that corresponds to an ideal capacitive response (−ZIm = (ωC)−1) (Fig. S8). MS plots of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 were performed (Fig. 6 a–c) which exhibit a positive slopes, suggesting all samples are n-type semiconductors. In addition, MS techniques is a reliable method for determining the flatband potential by measuring the photocurrent onset potential [44] and the concentration of charge carriers can also be detected by the MS plots, which are conducted to measure capacitance vs. applied potential at a fixed frequency,
(4) 1/C2 = (2/eεε 0Nd)[Ea − EFB − kT/e]

C is the space charge layer capacitance, ε and ε 0 is the dielectric constant of the material and permittivity in vacuum, e and Nd is the elemental charge and concentration of charge carriers, Ea and EFB is the applied potential and flat band potential, k and T is the Boltzmann's constant and temperature [44]. The result indicates that the slope of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 was ∼2/3 and ∼1/7 of that Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 at 300 Hz, respectively, which implies an increase of charge carrier density and a higher photocurrent density. The flatband potential (EFB) of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 are 0.280, 0.265, and 0.250 V vs. RHE, respectively. These potentials matched the obtained values in Fig. 5a and are very close to the photocurrent onset potential for sulfite oxidation by LSV (Table 2) [16].

Furthermore, to evaluate the charge transport performance of all samples, the photogenerated electron transport time (τ) was investigated. From the surface of photoanode to the substrate side, τ is determined by the controlled intensity modulated photocurrent spectrometer (CIMPS) measurements as the standard equations [45]:
(5)
τ = 1/2πf
where f is the frequency corresponding to the minimum of the imaginary components (Fig. 7 a–c) and τ is the electron transport time from the surface of photoanode to the substrate [45]. As shown in Fig. 7d, τ is a function of the incident flux (λ = 365 nm) for all samples. The electron transport time values of Bi2Mo2O9, Bi2MoO6, and Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 at 300 W m−2 are 0.087, 0.052, and 0.038 ms respectively, indicating that Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 has shorter transport time, higher charge collection efficiency and faster oxidation kinetics. Consistently, the Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 presents a better PEC performance compared with Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9, which is summarized in Table 2.

To further evaluate the charge transfer process behaviour at electrode/electrolyte interfaces, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at an open circuit potential under illumination. The radius of the arc on the EIS Nyquist plot reflects the reaction rate occurring at the electrode/electrolyte interfaces. As shown in Fig. S9, the semicircle arc for Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 was smaller than that of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9, indicating enhanced interfacial charge transfer to the electron donor/acceptor occurred, thus resulting faster water oxidation kinetics. It further confirms that the matched energy levels with Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 hybrid structure would not only enhance the driving force for electron transfer but also can improve the holes' transfer abilities from Bi2Mo2O9 to Bi2MoO6.

Based on the above discussion, a charge separation and transfer mechanism for Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 could be proposed in Fig. 8 . As an n-type semiconductor, the flat band potential (vs. RHE) is equal to its Fermi levels, which are 0.280 and 0.265 eV for Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6. In general, the bottom of the conduction band potential (ECB) of the n-type semiconductor is more negative (about 0.1 eV) than the Fermi levels, which is dependent on the electron effective mass and carrier concentration [10,46]. Hence, the ECB of Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 are 0.180 and 0.165 eV. On the base of the Eg tested by the derived Tauc plots (Fig. 3), the valence band potential (EVB) of Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 are 3.380 and 2.805 eV respectively according to the empirical formula (Eg = EVB − ECB). The considerable enhanced photocurrent of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 could be predominantly attributed to the optimized of the energy band alignment between Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6, and the matched energy levels for light-harvesting centers and catalytic sites would enlarge the driving force for electron/hole transfer. In the process of PEC system, photogenerated electron-hole pairs are generated by Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 under illumination. In detail, the EVB of Bi2Mo2O9 (3.380 eV) is more positive than that of Bi2MoO6 (2.805 eV), and holes can migrate smoothly to the valance band of Bi2MoO6; then transfer to the electrode/electrolyte interfaces to process a water oxidation reaction. While the ECB of Bi2MoO6 (0.165 eV) is negative than that of Bi2Mo2O9 (0.180 eV), electrons can transfer from Bi2MoO6 to the conduction band of Bi2Mo2O9, then reach the FTO substrate under the extra bias and flow to the counter electrode by external circuit to process a water reduction reaction, the energy band alignment between Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6 thus hindering photogenerated electron-hole recombination in Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9, which results in the enhancement of photocatalytic properties under illumination.

In this work, we demonstrated a SILAR process for one step fabrication of the hybrid phase Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9, which are controllably fabrication and verified successfully for the first time. Water oxidation measurements clearly reveal that Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanode attains a significantly enhanced photocurrent of 0.160 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under illumination, which is about 8.0 and 3.6 times higher than pristine Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6. Moreover, Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 photoanode with an enhanced charge separation efficiency (20.5%) and oxidation kinetics (54.6%) compared with pristine Bi2MoO6 and Bi2Mo2O9 at 1.23 V vs. RHE was obtained. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra and Mott-Schottky analysis, the considerable enhanced performance of Bi2MoO6@Bi2Mo2O9 could be predominantly attributed to the matched energy band between Bi2Mo2O9 and Bi2MoO6, which could not only improve the charge separation, but also accelerate the charge transfer. The optimized hybrid structure indicates an effective technique for fabricating other bimetallic oxide with Mo-based materials by SILAR method.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Chongqing University Postgraduates' Innovation Project (2017), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41702037) and Applied Basic Research Programs of Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (2017JY0146).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1 mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.01.090.
